results.title: Spatial - Alteryx Documentation
results.content:
This tool decreases the number of nodes that make up a polygon or polyline.

## Heat Map Tool

Green icon with glob and a fire symbol inside.

This tool uses individual records to generate polygons representing heat levels in an area..

## Make Grid Tool

Green icon with grid pattern.

This tool takes a specified spatial object and creates a grid based on the spatial object.

## Non Overlapping Drivetime Tool

Green icon with a badge and a car on the badge.

This tool creates drivetime trade areas, that do not overlap, for a point file.

## Poly-Build Tool

Green icon with white hexagon inside. That hexagon has arrows pointing to a white circle inside the hexagon.

This tool uses spacial point objects to draw polygons or polylines in a specific sort order.

## Poly-Split Tool [...] ## Spatial Process Tool

Spatial Process Tool Icon

The Spatial Process performs high-level spatial object editing from a simple, single tool.

## Trade Area Tool

Trade Area Tool Icon

Creates regions around specified point objects in the input file.

## Search results

No results found [...] ## Poly-Split Tool

Green icon with white hexagon inside and a circle inside the hexagon. The circle has arrows pointing from it to the outer hexagon.

This tool splits polygon or polyline objects into their component point, line, or region objects.

## Smooth Tool

Green icon containing a shape with smooth edges.

Uses a smoothing algorithm to round off the sharp angles of polygon or polyline objects.

## Spatial Info Tool

Green compass with question mark in the middle

The Spatial Info tool extracts tabular information about the spatial object.

## Spatial Match Tool

Spatial Match Icon

Establishes the spatial relationship between two sets of spatial objects.

## Spatial Process Tool

Spatial Process Tool Icon

results.title: ‎Scenery – Spatial Experiences App - App Store
results.content:
GravitasDarkMatter

  Having spent 20 years in the computer graphics industry working with similar tools, I can say Scenery is top-notch. It brings the simplicity and elegance of Apple’s user experience to the complex world of spatial creation. The tool is comprehensive — it feels like a Maya or Blender-style environment sitting on top of the dense RealityKit codebase — yet it makes building spatial experiences incredibly accessible for artists.One of the best aspects is that users who consume your creations require no installs. You can just share a link, and people can experience the spatial scene directly on iPhone, iPad, and even Vision Pro. That frictionless approach makes Scenery a standout for anyone serious about spatial content.
 ### Great tool for designers

  UserAbility [...] UserAbility

  As a designer, this is the quickest and easiest creation tool for the Apple Vision Pro. Importing models and wiring up interactions is simple. Experiences update real time so you can make a change and then try it in the headset seconds later. So glad to see creation tools like this coming to Apple Vision.
 ### Interactive AR prototyping!!!

  UserAbility

  This is the best tool I found for creating fast interactive prototypes as a designer. It’s easy to use and it’s being updated and improved quickly. This is the best AR creation tool that I know of right now.
 ### Artist and Educator

  BROWNTECK [...] BROWNTECK

  This app is amazing and allows artist to create mind awesome experiences! This is also a very powerful tool for education and storytelling. Great value and great app support.

– SharePlay Support View immersive experiences together with friends and family through FaceTime. Interactions and events are synchronized in real-time. – Volume Mode Launch experiences in volumes so they can coexist alongside other apps and windows. – Editor Improvements Orientation gizmo, better object orbiting and orthographic camera support. – Custom Lighting Environments Set the perfect lighting and reflections for your scene with custom environment maps alongside our default options. – Stability & Performance Bug fixes, UI improvements, and performance optimizations.

Version 1.1.19

results.title: What Is Spatial Data? A Beginner’s Guide
results.content: Working with spatial data requires the right tools, and the good news is you don't need expensive software to get started. There's a powerful ecosystem of open source tools that can handle everything from basic mapping to large scale geospatial processing. If you're looking for a desktop system, QGIS is the best place to start. It's fully featured, highly customizable, and free. Whether you're visualizing layers or performing spatial joins, or running even advanced geospatial processing, QGIS can handle it. For a spatial database, there's PostGIS, an extension of PostgreSQL, that brings powerful spatial capabilities to SQL. Need to find all the houses within a flood zone? Query millions of points to find the nearest location? PostGIS makes it possible with simple SQL statements. Now, if [...] which buildings are inside flood prone zones. Spatial relationships go beyond just proximity. Containment is a property inside a designated tax zone. Intersection. Do two areas overlap, like a conservation zone and a new construction project? Nearest neighbor. What's the closest hospital to an accident site? Entire industries run on these relationships. Retail chains optimize store locations based on customer density. Insurance companies assess risk by analyzing past disasters. Ridesharing apps match drivers and passengers in real time. Spatial thinking isn't just about looking at maps. It's about asking better questions and finding deeper insights. And once you start thinking spatially, you'll never look at data the same way again. Working with spatial data requires the right tools, and [...] If you're a data professional and you're not thinking about location, you're missing out on an entire dimension of insight. The where in your data is just as important as the what, and it's time to start using it. So if you want to dive deeper into the world of spatial analysis, modern GIS tools, and real-world applications, follow along because the future of data isn't just big, it's spatial.

results.title: What is Spatial Computing UX? A Complete Guide (2026) - Onething Design
results.content:
Apple Mindfulness uses immersive visuals, calming spatial audio, and minimal interaction to create a focused, distraction-free environment. It exemplifies how spatial UX can be used for emotional and cognitive well-being by reducing sensory overload and enhancing user presence.

## Tools Used in Spatial Computing UX Design

The spatial UX design toolchain is evolving rapidly, but a core set of tools has emerged that most practitioners rely on:

Do note that the choice of tools depends heavily on the platform being designed for, the fidelity of the prototype required, and the technical expertise of the design team.

## Industries Benefiting from Spatial Computing UX [...] ## What are the Challenges in Spatial Computing UX Design?

While spatial computing UX unlocks powerful new interaction models, it also introduces a unique set of challenges that do not exist in traditional screen-based design. Understanding these constraints is essential for designing practical, user-friendly spatial applications that can succeed in real-world conditions.

### 1. Hardware Limitations

Spatial computing devices, such as AR glasses and VR headsets, are still evolving, with constraints around field of view, battery life, processing power, and comfort. These limitations directly affect how much content can be displayed, how long users can engage, and how responsive interactions feel.

### 2. User Adoption Barriers [...] ### 3. Respect Depth and Scale

Objects in spatial computing should behave as they would in physical space. Text at a distance should be larger and interactive elements should have a sense of physical weight and resistance. Users have well-calibrated intuitions about the physical world, and violating those intuitions creates discomfort and confusion.

### 5. Build Inclusive Interactions

Not every user can perform precise hand gestures or move freely through a space. Spatial UX design must account for a wide range of physical abilities, and always offer multiple input pathways (gesture, voice, gaze, and controller) so no user is excluded by their interaction method of choice.

Also Read: Agentic UX Design - 5 Patterns That Work

## Top 4 Successful Spatial Computing UX Examples

results.title: Design for spatial user interfaces - WWDC23 - Videos
results.content: depth to create hierarchy. Ornaments are a great way to present toolbars. They allow people to perform quick actions related to the content in a convenient location. And they add a bit of depth to your app. Since ornaments are usually a collection of buttons and sit on their own glass container, this is a perfect place to use borderless buttons. In this case, it's clear that these are interactive elements, and they'll still get the hover effect as people look at them. Here's another great use of an ornament in the Music app. The Now Playing controls are presented as an ornament that persists no matter where you are in the app. This allows people to search for their next song while being able to control their music. When ornaments sit at the bottom edge of the window, place them so they [...] spatial. Lorena: Thanks, Miquel. Let's start by exploring some of the best ways to position content so that it feels comfortable. Then show you how to properly size content so that it's easy to target. And lastly, show you how to create layouts that work with our new and beautiful eye focus feedback. Because people primarily interact with their eyes and hands on this platform, it's important to create experiences that prioritize people's physical comfort and safety. More than ever, you need to consider the ergonomics of your design, ensuring that the placement of content is intentional and doesn't cause eye or neck fatigue. For example, because of the neck's range of motion, it's easier for most people to turn their head farther to the right and to the left rather than up and down. So [...] not only visually appealing, but also familiar and easy to use in this new platform. All our system components have been designed to support our wide array of inputs, while maintaining consistency with the rest of the Apple ecosystem so you, as a designer or developer, can focus on generating groundbreaking immersive and spatial experiences. Thanks for watching. Both: Ciao! ♪
